story:
Brent watched Vera leave, her ponytail bouncing left to right as she shrank toward her car. They had met at a UFO convention two years ago, trading heart-pounding tales: her three triangular lights hovering in a park under a periwinkle sky, his saucer sighting over the western mountains during a lunar eclipse. Now it was over. Her belief had thinned, and his fervor felt suddenly lonely. He drifted to the kitchen, sat on a counter stool, and stared at his cell, volleying should-I-or-shouldn’t-I into the air.

A royal-blue droplet, crystalline and impossible, appeared on the locked screen. He reached to wipe it away. Words bloomed under his fingertip: “Tonight, at dusk. Kerrigan’s Farm. Seeing is believing.” His eyes widened like silver dollars. The message dissolved as mysteriously as it had arrived. Kerrigan’s Farm had been vacant for years, but the line between skepticism and hope wavered, and he was a sucker for wonder. After a nap that felt like sinking into a tug-of-war between sorrow, disbelief, and anxious anticipation, he headed out at sunset.

He serpentined along a dirt path to the abandoned silo and found a scatter of vehicles in the field, parked at odd angles. He pulled up perpendicular to a multicolored microbus and scanned the faces. Some wore elaborate alien costumes that drew side-eyed snickers. He recognized an eccentric aficionado from a past convention, his joke just forming when a humming vibration spilled from the star-littered sky. Heads tilted upward. A saucer slid into view, spinning slowly as if measuring the land for safety. Fifty feet across, hovering two hundred feet up, it traced a soft halo of sepia-toned laser light around a seamless frame.

Ten light-blue droplets unfurled beneath it. An echoing gong tolled. The droplets cascaded to the grass, splashed quietly, and from each pool rose an alien—human-like faces softly blue and illuminated, eyes gentle. A sincere voice carried from the craft: “Humanoids, our visit from planet Neptune is of peace and brevity; we can only endure Earth’s atmosphere a short time. We have come to reward you for your undying belief. To show goodwill, we shall entertain you with music common to our planet.”

A serene chorus in an unknown dialect threaded with invisible instruments washed over the field. Each visitor danced among the crowd, their cadence like an orchestra of voice and machine tuned to tranquility. Brent let his head tip back, lost, when a rubbery, ridged hand slid into his and guided him toward a silhouette. Vera stood there, one eye tucked behind brown hair, an inviting, changed light in her face. “I got the message too. I now believe in them—and especially you,” she said, and dove into his arms. They swayed with the others, breathing the same quiet rhythm.

The music ebbed. The aliens returned to their splashdown circles, placed hands over hearts, waved, and re-formed into droplets. They rose, rejoined the ship, and the saucer itself faded into the night. A smattering of applause; a single voice: “Will anyone believe this?” Everyone’s phones were dead. Proof had been switched off.

Vera tightened her embrace and whispered, “It doesn’t matter. We believe… together.”
